sections.title: What it is

On this surface, the issue usually develops because scuff marks are often rubber or metal transfer on the sealer or polish, not embedded stone staining. A useful professional clue is that if abrasion cuts through sealer, stop and reassess repair options. Technically, neutral cleaning removes transfer films without etching calcite minerals or stripping sealer unevenly.

sections.title: Why it happens
sections.content: Scuff marks are often rubber or metal transfer on the sealer or polish, not embedded stone staining. This problem falls into the damage category, which matters because the removal strategy changes based on whether the issue is active soil, mineral loading, microbial film, or true surface damage. Professional context: - If abrasion cuts through sealer, stop and reassess repair options. Neutral cleaning removes transfer films without etching calcite minerals or stripping sealer unevenly.

sections.title: Where it appears
sections.content: On granite, scratches commonly appears in areas exposed to repeated use, moisture, or residue buildup. It is most often seen in kitchens, bathrooms, and high-contact zones where contaminants accumulate over time.

IF: finish risk is elevated, stop and reassess before adding stronger chemistry or heavier abrasion.
